Source_Species.reason: SmLAC1 is a gene from Salvia miltiorrhiza throughout the paper.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The species name Salvia miltiorrhiza is explicit.

Applied_Species.reason: SmLAC1 was functionally tested in transgenic S. miltiorrhiza.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied species is explicitly Salvia miltiorrhiza.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: SmLAC1 is a laccase; the paper describes LACs as multicopper oxidoreductases.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Results state SmLAC1 was predominantly expressed in flowers and later tested by overexpression/knockout in leaves.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The study places SmLAC1 in PA biosynthesis/polymerization within the flavonoid pathway.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Results show SmLAC1 catalyzes PA polymerization and B-type-to-A-type conversion.

Primary_Substrate.reason: In vitro assays directly used (+)-catechin, (-)-epicatechin, procyanidin B1, B2, and B3 as substrates.

Primary_Product.reason: Products identified include procyanidins B1, B2, B3, A1, A2, plus other oxidized oligomers.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: SmLAC1 acts in polymerization of monomers into PAs, a downstream/terminal step.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: Direct enzymatic formation of polymerized PAs and conversion of B-type to A-type was shown.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The Discussion proposes LAC catalysis as a main mechanism/main control of PA formation, but 'rate-limiting' was not directly tested.

Enzyme_Name.reason: SmLAC1 is explicitly named as laccase 1.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number for SmLAC1/laccase is stated in the paper text.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The study directly measures proanthocyanidins including procyanidins A1, A2, B1, B2, and B3.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: PAs are described as flavonoids/proanthocyanidins (condensed tannins).

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: PA effects for SmLAC1 were examined in S. miltiorrhiza flowers and leaves.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The introduction explicitly lists plant protective roles and multiple health-benefit activities of PAs.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Overexpression increased PAs and decreased catechin/epicatechin, while knockout showed the opposite.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are reported: recombinant assays, UPLC/Q-TOF-MS/MS, NMR, overexpression, CRISPR knockout, and metabolite profiling.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: This matches the reported in vitro activities of polymerizing catechin/epicatechin into B-type products and converting B-type to A-type.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each listed point is described in the Results/Discussion, with direct experimental support.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Application as a target to increase PA accumulation is a reasonable inference, but stacking with upstream genes and specific utility in woody crops/astringency are not shown in the paper.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Reduced monomers is supported and no obvious phenotype is reported for some transgenics, but sensory/astringency and carbon-allocation tradeoffs were not tested.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Plants were greenhouse-grown and in vitro enzyme assays were performed in acetate buffer at pH 5.0.

Omics_Data.reason: NCBI BioProject PRJNA1210854 and Science Data Bank DOI 10.57760/sciencedb.19944 are explicitly provided.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Both monomers as starter/extension units and substrate preference statements are reported; epicatechin favored among monomers and B2 noted as more favorable among B-type substrates.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: SmLAC1 is stated to be predominantly/highly expressed in S. miltiorrhiza flowers.

corrections.reason: No EC number for SmLAC1/laccase is stated in the paper text.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The Latin name Salvia miltiorrhiza is explicit.

Applied_Species.reason: The gene was examined in S. miltiorrhiza miR397a-overexpressing flowers by qRT-PCR.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied species is Salvia miltiorrhiza.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: SmLAC23 is one of the SmLAC laccase genes; LACs are described as multicopper oxidoreductases.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Fig. 1c shows SmLAC23 downregulated in flowers of miR397a-overexpressing plants.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The paper links downregulated SmLAC targets collectively to PA polymerization, but SmLAC23 itself was not individually functionally assigned to PA biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: PA polymerization is inferred for the targeted SmLAC subset, but not directly tested for SmLAC23 alone.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: A downstream polymerization role is inferred from being an SmLAC target, not individually demonstrated.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: Only a likely contribution as part of the miR397a-targeted LAC subset can be inferred; no direct evidence for SmLAC23 alone.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The value says not individually established, which matches the paper.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number is provided for SmLAC23 in the paper.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: PAs are the affected metabolite class in the transgenics, but SmLAC23 is not directly tied individually to PA production.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Proanthocyanidins are described as flavonoid/condensed tannin metabolites in the study context.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: The measured association is in flowers.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: These general PA functions are stated in the introduction.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: SmLAC23 downregulation is shown in miR397a-overexpressors, which also have reduced PAs and increased catechin/epicatechin.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: qRT-PCR plus association with PA metabolite profiling were indeed used.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: The summary accurately reflects that SmLAC23 was downregulated in the context of lowered LAC activity and altered PA/monomer levels, without individual biochemical testing.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is speculative application not directly discussed or tested.

Omics_Data.reason: RNA-seq and BioProject PRJNA1210854 are reported for the transgenic flower transcriptome.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Calling SmLAC23 'core' is too strong; it was specifically measured, but the developer criteria require direct testing in Results, which was not done individually.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: It is shown in flowers in qRT-PCR context, but a general 'flower-expressed' source-species pattern for SmLAC23 alone is not clearly established beyond that assay.

corrections.reason: No EC number is provided for SmLAC23 in the paper.

corrections.old_value: Core because explicitly quantified in Results as a miR397a-regulated LAC associated with PA changes, though not biochemically validated individually

Source_Species.reason: The study used Ptr-MIR397a from Populus trichocarpa, and mature S. miltiorrhiza miR397 is identical to it.

Applied_Species.reason: Ptr-MIR397a was overexpressed in both Salvia miltiorrhiza and Populus trichocarpa.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Both Latin names are explicit in the paper.

Regulator_Type.reason: miR397a is a microRNA/small noncoding RNA acting post-transcriptionally.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper states mature S. miltiorrhiza miR397 is a 21-nt sequence identical to P. trichocarpa miR397a and MIR397 is conserved.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Its levels were experimentally elevated in transgenic S. miltiorrhiza flowers and transgenic P. trichocarpa was generated/tested in leaves.

Metabolic_Process_Controlled.reason: The study concludes miR397a negatively regulates PA biosynthesis/polymerization via laccases.

Primary_Regulatory_Targets.reason: SmLAC1/23/24/32 and additional targeted SmLACs are supported; PtLAC13 and PtLAC37-39/PtLAC48 are mentioned only in Discussion as orthologs/targets and were not directly tested here.

Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes.reason: Overexpression reduced target SmLAC transcript levels and total LAC activity.

Regulation_Mode.reason: The paper describes miRNAs as mainly acting through direct cleavage of transcripts with near-perfect complementarity; prior work identified SmLAC targets.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The relevant terminal nutrient/metabolite products are PAs.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Effects were measured in S. miltiorrhiza flowers and P. trichocarpa leaves.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: General functions/health benefits of PAs are stated in the introduction.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: miR397a overexpression reduced polymerized PAs and increased catechin/epicatechin in both species.

Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product.reason: The paper explicitly concludes miR397a is a negative regulator of PA polymerization/biosynthesis through repression of LACs in both species.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Transgenic overexpression, qRT-PCR, LAC activity assay, metabolomics/UPLC, and RNA-seq were all used.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: These points are all supported by Results and concluding statements.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Using miR397a as an engineering lever is inferable, but pleiotropic deployment considerations are not experimentally tested in this paper.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: No obvious phenotype in this study is supported, and prior lignin effects are cited, but specific structural-trait tradeoffs were not tested here.

Omics_Data.reason: RNA-seq, metabolomics, and the stated database accessions/DOI are reported.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The mature S. miltiorrhiza miR397 sequence being identical to P. trichocarpa miR397a and use of Ptr-MIR397a in both species are explicit.

Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation.reason: The paper discusses possible indirect feedback from monomer accumulation on upstream genes and does not establish direct miR397a feedback.
